claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can assess your situation accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To determine if you're eligible, I need to ask you a few questions. Let me start with:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who is 16, and Blake, who is 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Now I need to understand your specific situation for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start with some basic questions:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your living arrangements with the children:

**Do both Alex (16) and Blake (5) currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, actually Alex doesn't live with me - he lives elsewhere. But Blake does live with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask about Alex first:

**Since Alex doesn't live with you, are you making any financial contributions towards his upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep - I help with things like clothes, food, and other expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To assess this properly:

**How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep, and how often do you make these contributions? Also, do you know how much Child Benefit is currently paid (approximately £25 per week for the eldest child)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep, so that's more than the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex's current situation since he's 16:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college, an apprenticeship, or other approved courses)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Now let me ask about Blake:

**Since Blake lives with you and is 5 years old, is there anyone else who is also claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or are you the only person responsible for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for him, and Blake lives with them primarily. I should clarify - Blake doesn't actually live with me, sorry for the confusion earlier.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important clarification. So to confirm I understand correctly:

**Blake lives with his other parent (who is already claiming Child Benefit for him). Do you make any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep, similar to what you do for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me make sure I have all the facts correct before making my assessment:

- **Alex (16)**: Lives elsewhere, you contribute £50/week towards his upkeep, and he's in approved education (A-levels)
- **Blake (5)**: Lives with his other parent who already claims Child Benefit for him, and you don't contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep

**Is there anything else about your situation with either child that I should know - for example, are either of them in hospital or care, or are there any other special circumstances?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's nothing else - neither of them are in hospital or care or anything like that. You've got all the details correct.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming all the details. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's my determination:

**For Alex (16): ✓ You ARE eligible**
Alex is in approved education (A-levels), which means Child Benefit can continue until age 20. Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £50 per week towards his upkeep - which is more than the Child Benefit amount. Under the rules, you're considered responsible for Alex through these financial contributions, so you qualify for Child Benefit for him.

**For Blake (5): ✗ You are NOT eligible**
Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. Since you don't live with Blake and aren't making financial contributions towards his upkeep, you're not considered responsible for him under the Child Benefit rules.

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge on any Child Benefit you receive.

If you'd like to claim Child Benefit for Alex, you can apply through the government's Child Benefit service. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and in approved education (A-levels), which means eligibility can continue until age 20 for children in approved education or training. Although Alex doesn't live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards his upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25/week for the eldest child). Under the rules, you can continue to get Child Benefit for a child who lives elsewhere if you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. You are responsible for Alex through your financial contributions, and you meet all the eligibility criteria.

Blake lives with his other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since you are not living with Blake and are not making any financial contributions towards his upkeep, you are not considered responsible for him under the Child Benefit rules. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has two children. For Alex (16), they are eligible because they contribute more than the Child Benefit amount towards his upkeep (£50/week) and Alex is in approved education. For Blake (5), they are not eligible because Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for him, and the claimant makes no financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep, meaning they are not considered responsible for Blake under the rules.

Under the rules, you can continue to get Child Benefit for a child who lives elsewhere if you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. You are responsible for Alex through your financial contributions, and you meet all the eligibility criteria.

Blake lives with his other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child. Since you are not living with Blake and are not making any financial contributions towards his upkeep, you are not considered responsible for him under the Child Benefit rules. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has two children. For Alex (16), they are eligible because they contribute more than the Child Benefit amount towards his upkeep (£50/week) and Alex is in approved education. For Blake (5), they are not eligible because Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for him, and the claimant makes no financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep, meaning they are not considered responsible for Blake under the rules.
